title: Basic logistic regression
text:
In this first section we will use the sample Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset BreastCancer. This contains a number of histological variables, plus a response varable: is the cell benign or malignant? Using this dataset we will learn how to:
You will need to run each code block in order or you’ll get some errors! You are welcome to chnage the code and experiment - everything here runs in your web browser. Some chunks may take some time to run.

title: Basic logistic regression
text:
Our dataset
For this tutorial we’ll be using the BreastCancer dataset from the mlbench package. Let’s load the BreastCancer dataset and take a quick look:















NoteHint 1





glimpse is a tool to preview a dataset. Use the name of the dataset inside glimpse()











TipFully worked solution:





Use the glimpse() function from dplyr:
data(BreastCancer, package = 'mlbench')
glimpse(BreastCancer)

Load the BreastCancer dataset from the mlbench package,
Use glimpse() to preview the data.





It looks like we have:

One ID column Id
9 predictors Cl.thickness, Cell.size, Cell.shape, Marg.adhesion, Epith.c.size, Bare.nuclei, Bl.cromatin Normal.nucleoli, Mitoses
A dichotymous output variable Class

We can explore this by creating a data table stratified by Class. To do this we’ll use the gtsummary packge and the function tbl_summary()

We don’t want Id included in our table, so we’ll exclude it first using dplyr’s select() function. We then build the table.
















NoteHint 1





dplyr::select allows us to remove fields from our dataset by prepending a variable (e.g. Id) with a ‘-’ sign











TipFully worked solution:





BreastCancer |>  #1
    dplyr::select(-Id) |>. #2
    gtsummary::tbl_summary(by = 'Class'). #3
Use the select() function from dplyr 1. The symbol ‘|>’ is a pipe - it passed the output from one function and passes it straight into the next. In the first line it simply passed the dataset BreastCancer into the next function. 2. Removes -Id column then data frame less the Id column is passed to the table builder function tbl_summary from the gtsummary package 3. Builds our table with separate columns for each class (benign/malignant)





Data type conversion
Most of our predictors are factors, but we’ll probably want to treat these as continuous variables from now on.
If we want to consider our predictors as continuous variables, we’ll need to change their type first. To do this we need to first convert them into character strings, then convert those into numbers. We can use dplyr’s `mutate function to do this:








This is a bit more complex than what we’ve done before. mutate() creates or changes variables. across() applys our custom function to any variables except ‘Class’. Our custom function takes each column, converts it to a character, then converts that to number. We need to do it like this because R treats factors differently.

title: Basic logistic regression
text:
Univariate analysis

We can now rebuild the table and add the add_p function at the end to run a univariate analysis on our predictors









These all look like really useful features for predicting cell malignancy even in a univariate analysis. This isn’t always the case, and even if a predictor performs poorly in a univariate analysis, it may still perform well in a multivariate analysis.

title: Basic logistic regression
text:
Logistic regression model
We can go ahead and build a simple logistic regression model with all these predictors.
We need to define a function which takes looks something like this: response_variable ~ predictor_variables The predictor_variables could be a list (e.g. Cell.size + Cell.shape + Mitoses), or we can use the wildcard . for all variables except the reponse variable.
Excercise: 1. Build and run a logistic regression model. Set data to BreastCancer and family to 'binomial' 2. Call summary() on our new model to get a summary of the model output















NoteHint 1





data = is our dataset family = should be binomial for logistic regression











TipFully worked solution:





bc_model_logistic <- # where we will store our model
    glm(Class ~ .,   # our formula
            data = BreastCancer, 
            family = 'binomial')

# Take a look at the model summary:
summary(bc_model_logistic)




Another way to display the regression results is to use the tbl_regression() from gtsummary. We’ll add the exponentiate = TRUE argument so we get odds ratios rather than log(odds) - this makes it much easier to interpret:

The extra line gtsummary::add_glance_source_note() adds some model performance details to the table caption. bold_p … I’m sure you can work out what that does.









And we can use the plot_model() function from sjPlot to quickly render a graph showing the effect sizes of each predictor:















NoteHint 1





Just call the name of your plot object to display it











TipFully worked solution:





library(sjPlot)

model_effect_plot <- bc_model_logistic |>
    sjPlot::plot_model()
    
model_effect_plot




Nice! That was pretty easy, wasn’t it?
Our model_effect_plot is a ggplot object, which means we can modify it, e.g.








We can get a rough estimate of how our model performs using performance.








A few important things to note about our model:

Some predictors don’t seem to be very good.
It assumes that all predictors have a linear response. If we look at the categorical data before we converted it to numbers, we can see that sometimes the responses rise, peak and then fall again. Our logistic regression model misses this.
Some variables correlate with each other. In a logistic regression, the effect is split across those variables and is diluted. We could be missing something important!
If we look at our model output we see:
(16 observations deleted due to missingness)

If any subject has any missing data, they get dropped from the analysis completely.
We will now look at how we can deal with 1, 2, 3 and 4.

We could simplify our model by removing ineffective predictors - there is a fine balance between gaining information from extra predictors and overfitting (model too complex). Removing predictors can effect the model, especially if there are cryptic associations with other variables. We’ll look at ways to optimise models later.
We could transform the data which shows a non-linear response.
We could remove correlated variables, keeping the ones with most information
Missing data can be a) ignored, and accept that we lose some data; b) treated by removing predictors with high levels of missingness; c) interpolated (e.g. replacing missings with mean values).

In the next stages we will look at feature selection, and model optimisation.
